intro:
Paul (Latin Paulus, "small"), known by his Hebrew name Saul until his missionary work among Greeks and Romans, was the apostle to the Gentiles and the most prolific author of the New Testament. Born in Tarsus of Cilicia into a devout Jewish family of the tribe of Benjamin, he was "a Hebrew of Hebrews" yet also a Roman citizen from birth — a dual standing that shaped his mission and his legal trials (Philippians 3:5; Acts 22:25–28). Raised "according to the strictest party" as a Pharisee and trained in Jerusalem under the eminent rabbi Gamaliel (Acts 22:3; Acts 26:5), he also learned the trade of tentmaking, by which he later supported himself (Acts 18:3).

Saul first appears in Scripture consenting to the stoning of Stephen and then "ravaging the church," a zealous persecutor convinced he served God (Acts 8:1–3; Galatians 1:13–14). On the road to Damascus the risen Christ confronted him in blinding light — "Saul, Saul, why do you persecute me?" — and in a moment turned the persecutor into a preacher (Acts 9:1–19). Paul understood this not as a change of religion but as a divine call, like the prophets of old, to proclaim the Son among the nations (Galatians 1:15–16). After a period in Arabia and Damascus and quiet years in Tarsus, he was brought by Barnabas to the church at Antioch, the base from which the Spirit sent him out (Acts 11:25–26; Acts 13:2–3).

Across three missionary journeys recorded in Acts 13–21, Paul planted churches through Cyprus, Asia Minor, Macedonia, and Greece, reasoning in synagogues and marketplaces from Antioch to Athens to Ephesus and Corinth. He defended the gospel of grace for Gentiles at the Jerusalem Council (Acts 15:1–29), gathered a collection for the poor saints in Jerusalem, and there was arrested, imprisoned two years in Caesarea, and — appealing to Caesar — sent by ship to Rome (Acts 21:27–33; Acts 25:11–12; Acts 28:16). His thirteen letters, from the doctrinal heights of Romans to the pastoral warmth of Philemon, range over justification, union with Christ, the indwelling Spirit, the church as Christ's body, and the resurrection hope, and were received by the church as Scripture (2 Peter 3:15–16).

Paul's theological legacy is immense. He articulated, more fully than any other writer, justification by faith apart from works of the law (Romans 3:28; Galatians 2:16), the believer's death and new life in Christ, and the breaking down of the dividing wall between Jew and Gentile in one new people (Ephesians 2:14–16). Yet his gospel was forged in weakness: he gloried not in his achievements but in the cross and in his own frailty, that "the power of Christ" might rest upon him (2 Corinthians 12:9–10). Traditionally martyred at Rome under Nero, he could write near the end, "I have fought the good fight, I have finished the race, I have kept the faith" (2 Timothy 4:7). He remains the towering figure of the apostolic age — the pattern of grace shown to the chief of sinners (1 Timothy 1:15–16) and the great theologian of the new covenant.
